question:
How many credits are there in Applied Machine Learning
  in Health Care?
answer:
There are 3 credits in Applied Machine Learning in Health Care.
This information is supported by multiple contexts, including the course title and credits listed in Context 1 and Context 2, and the course code and credits mentioned in Context 3.
The credits are also consistent across all contexts, including Context 4 and Context 5, which provide additional details about the course.
This information is available in the uploaded handouts, specifically 2025_course_handout_AMCH.pdf.

Course: Applied Machine Learning in Health Care
Course Code: MTH3705
Credits: 3
Section: Document Overview
Section Type: general

Even Semester AY 2025-26 Page 1 of 8 ©BMU Course Code: MTH3705 Course Name: Applied Machine Learning in Health Care Credits: 3-0-0 Contact hours per week: 3 lectures per week Batch: 2023, 6th Sem Academic Year: 2025-26 Semester Duration: 19th Jan - 05th May 2026 Instructor-in-charge: Dr Palak Goel Email – palak.goel@bmu.edu.in Phone -7838887146 Office –23, Fourth Floor, E2 Building Course Coordinator – Dr Palak Goel Email – palak.goel@bmu.edu.in Phone -7838887146 Office –23, Fourth Floor, E2 Building Aim of the course: In the post-COVID-19 era, understanding disease dynamics, analyzing epidemiological data, and building predictive models have become crucial skills for engineers working in healthcare technology, data science, public health informatics, and AI-driven health solutions. This course is designed to equip B.Tech. students with practical skills in epidemic modeling, data analysis, and visualization using Python. Course Overview: This is an introductory undergraduate course on epidemic modeling and infectious disease data analysis using Python. The focus will be on epidemic modeling and infectious disease data analysis using Python, with an emphasis on combining theoretical understanding and hands-on programming. The course covers the analysis and visualization of real-world epidemiological datasets, development of compartmental disease models, and application of statistical and machine learning techniques for prediction and forecasting. Salient topics of the course are listed below. Week-Wise Schedule: Note: Weekly course schedule includes 03 lecture sessions. Week 1-3: Introduction to Public Health Data, Epidemiological Data Wrangling, and Analysis Understanding "Line lists," incidence vs. prevalence, and the "Chain of Infection", Fetching live data, Handling biological data, Pivot tables for time-series structure, Moving averages to smooth daily infection spikes. Incidence mapping and choropleth maps. Week 4-7: The Physics of Pandemics (Compartmental Models) and Parameter Estimation Introduction to epidemic modeling (mechanistic vs phenomenological), Compartmental Models, Parameter estimation: least squares and maximum likelihood, Basic reproduction number (R0) and herd immunity, Effective reproduction number (Rt), Model validation, Implementing and simulating compartmental models in Python. Week 8-10: Time Series & Forecasting Introduction to time series analysis, Time series objects and data structures, Stationarity and differencing, Trend estimation and smoothing, Seasonal decomposition (additive and multiplicative), Autocorrelation and partial autocorrelation, Simple forecasting methods (naive, moving average, drift), ARIMA models, Model selection, Cross-validation for time series, Residual diagnostics and forecast accuracy metrics Even Semester AY 2025-26 Page 2 of 8 ©BMU Week 11-12: Regression and Classification Methods Linear Regression, Multiple Linear Regression, Polynomial regression, Logistic Regression, Poisson regression using public health data. Week 13-16: Ensemble Modelling Introduction to neural networks; Architecture: neurons, layers, activation functions; Forward propagation and backpropagation; Overfitting and regularization techniques; Applications to epidemic forecasting. Course Outcomes: At the end of the course, the students should be able to: Course Outcome Statement Mapped Component Associated Activities / Assessment CO1: Understand the fundamentals of public health data and epidemiological concepts including line lists, incidence and prevalence measures, chain of infection, biological data handling, time-series structuring, smoothing techniques, and spatial visualization using incidence maps and choropleth maps. Skill Development Quiz, Assignment, Mid-Term Presentation & End-Term Presentation for project work CO2: Apply data wrangling,

Understanding of Concepts (40%) ● Epidemiological concepts such as incidence, prevalence, compartmental models, and time-series basics. ● Validation of students’ understanding through timed quiz. Problem Solving (60%) ● Application of data analysis, modeling, and forecasting techniques. Even Semester AY 2025-26 Page 6 of 8 ©BMU ● Logical ability to problem-solving skill in a timely manner. Assignment 20 (1 assignment of weightage 20%) April 10, 2026 One open-book assignment of weight 10%. Understanding of Concepts (20%) ● Detailed understanding of epidemiological data structures, modeling assumptions, and forecasting methods. ● Ability to interpret outputs from computational tools. Timeliness (20%) ● Assignment must be submitted on or before the due date and time. Problem Solving (60%) ● Application of computational and statistical methods to real-world public health datasets. ● Interpretation and discussion of analytical results. Mid-Term Presentation (Equivalent to Mid-Term Examination) 20 March 29, 2026 (Presentation tentatively between March 30-April 03 2026) Understanding of Concepts (20%) ● Clarity in explaining epidemiological concepts, data sources, and modeling approach. ● Ability to relate theory with real-world disease data. Timeliness (10%) ● Presentation submission on or before the specified deadline. Problem Solving (20%) ● Demonstration of coding skills. ● Visualization of the problems using software. Presentation and Viva (50%) ● Logical structure, clarity of slides, and effective communication. ● Developing presentation skills to present the work in a group. ● Demonstration of individual knowledge through viva. Project 40 April 22 2026 Understanding of Concepts (10%) Even Semester AY 2025-26 Page 7 of 8 ©BMU (Equivalent to End-Term Examination) (Presentation tentative April 23 -May 1 2026) ● Comprehensive understanding of epidemiological theory and computational techniques. Timeliness (10%) ● Submission of project report and code within the stipulated deadline. Report Writing and Problem Solving (30%) ● Preparation of a structured technical report. ● Application of modeling, forecasting, and data analysis methods to a real disease dataset. ● Demonstration of programming skills and result interpretation. Presentation and Viva (50%) ● Clear presentation of methodology, results, and conclusions. ● Demonstration of individual contribution and understanding through viva. ● Ability to apply concepts to solve complex problems Experiential Learning Components: Hands-on projects on applications of Computational Epidemiology and Public Health Data Analytics using Python will be given to the students. Students will work in groups on real-world public health datasets involving epidemiological data wrangling, implementation of compartmental epidemic models, parameter estimation, time-series forecasting, and visualization of disease trends through dashboards. This entire set of experiential learning activities will be assessed through the evaluation component carrying a weightage of 60%. Student Responsibilities: ● Attend lectures to obtain all the course material that you are responsible for. ● Check announcements at Maitri/Google Classroom on a regular basis. ● Submit assignments/projects on time. Regularly, check your marks on the Maitri and inform the instructor in-charge in case of any issues. Attendance Policy: Students are expected to attend the classes regularly. C2 Epidemiological Data Wrangling & Visualization - Fetching live epidemiological data - Data cleaning and preprocessing - Pivot tables and time-series restructuring - Moving averages and smoothing - Incidence mapping and choropleth maps CO1, CO2, CO4 U AP EV 6 C3 Physics of Pandemics & Compartmental Models - Mechanistic vs phenomenological models - SIR, SEIR and extensions - Model assumptions and limitations - Numerical simulation of compartmental models. CO1, CO2, CO3, CO4 U AP AN EV 9 C4 Parameter Estimation & Reproduction Numbers - Least squares estimation - Maximum likelihood estimation - Basic reproduction number (R₀) - Effective reproduction number (Rₜ) - Herd immunity threshold and model validation CO1, CO2, CO3, CO4 U AP AN EV 9 C5 Time Series Analysis in Epidemiology - Time series concepts and data structures - Stationarity and differencing - Trend estimation and smoothing - Seasonal decomposition CO1, CO2, CO4 U AP 6 C6 Forecasting Models for Disease Spread - Autocorrelation and partial autocorrelation - Naive, moving average and drift methods - ARIMA models - Model selection and cross-validation - Residual diagnostics and forecast accuracy metrics CO1, CO2, CO3, CO4 U AP AN EV 6 C7 Regression and Neural Networks for Public Health - Linear, logistic and Poisson regression - Neural network architecture and training - Overfitting and regularization - Epidemic forecasting using neural networks - Model development and final project CO1, CO2, CO3, CO4 U AP AN EV 6 Total contact hours=48 Even Semester AY 2025-26 Page 5 of 8 ©BMU Learning Resources: Textbooks: 1. Ding, Peng. Epidemic Modeling and Analysis with R. CRC Press, 2023. (Python adaptations will be provided for all R code examples). Reference Books: 1. McKinney, Wes. Python for Data Analysis: Data Wrangling with Pandas, NumPy, and Jupyter. 3rd Edition, O’Reilly Media, 2022. 2. VanderPlas, Jake. Python Data Science Handbook: Essential Tools for Working with Data. O’Reilly Media, 2016. 3. Keeling, Matt J., and Pejman Rohani. Modeling Infectious Diseases in Humans and Animals. Princeton University Press, 2008. 4. Vynnycky, Emilia, and Richard G. White. An Introduction to Infectious Disease Modelling. Oxford University Press, 2010. E-Learning (and other) Resources: 1. https://www.statsmodels.org/stable/tsa.html 2. https://people.duke.edu/~rnau/411arim.htm 3. https://www.youtube.com/c/statquest 4. https://www.who.int/data 5. https://www.kaggle.com/datasets?search=epidemiology Assessment Pattern: The final grade will be based on the marks obtained in various assessment components defined in the assessment table below. Relative grading method defined in the academic regulations of the university will be followed to grade the students.
